title: Architecture Decision Record: API Gateway Selection
author: Mike Johnson
content:
Architecture Decision Record: API Gateway Selection

Status: Accepted
Date: 2025-11-15
Deciders: John Smith, Sarah Chen, Mike Johnson

Context:
Project Atlas requires a centralized API gateway for routing, authentication, rate limiting, and monitoring. We evaluated three options:

1. Kong (Open Source) - Self-managed, highly customizable
2. AWS API Gateway - Managed service, tight AWS integration
3. Nginx Plus - Proven performance, simpler configuration

Decision:
We chose Kong because:
- Flexibility for both cloud and on-premise deployment
- Rich plugin ecosystem (OAuth, rate limiting, logging)
- Active community and commercial support available
- Better fit for our hybrid cloud strategy

Consequences:
- Team needs Kong expertise (training planned)
- We manage the infrastructure ourselves
- More control over configuration and customization
- Can migrate to any cloud provider without lock-in

title: Best Practices: Microservices Communication
author: Sarah Chen
content:
Best Practices: Microservices Communication

Synchronous Communication:
- Use REST APIs for request-response patterns
- Always set timeouts (default: 5 seconds)
- Implement circuit breakers for external calls
- Use correlation IDs for distributed tracing

Asynchronous Communication:
- Use message queues (RabbitMQ/SQS) for fire-and-forget
- Use event streaming (Kafka) for event-driven patterns
- Ensure idempotent message processing
- Implement dead letter queues for failed messages

Service Discovery:
- Use Kubernetes DNS for internal service discovery
- Kong upstream configuration for external routing
- Health check endpoints required for all services

Error Handling:
- Use standard error response format across all services
- Include correlation ID in error responses
- Log errors with structured logging (JSON format)
- Never expose internal errors to external consumers

Security:
- mTLS for all service-to-service communication
- OAuth tokens for user-facing APIs
- API keys for third-party integrations
- Network policies to restrict pod-to-pod communication

title: Technical Documentation: OAuth 2.0 Implementation Guide
author: Sarah Chen
content:
OAuth 2.0 Implementation Guide

Overview:
This guide covers the OAuth 2.0 implementation for Project Atlas, including setup, configuration, and integration instructions.

Token Flow:
1. Client requests authorization code via /auth/authorize
2. User authenticates and grants consent
3. Client exchanges code for tokens via /auth/token
4. Access token used in Authorization header
5. Refresh token used to get new access tokens

Token Specifications:
- Access Token: JWT, RSA-256 signed, 1-hour expiry
- Refresh Token: Opaque, stored in Redis, 24-hour sliding expiry
- ID Token: JWT with user claims

Scopes:
- read: Read-only access to resources
- write: Read-write access to resources
- admin: Administrative operations
- service: Service-to-service (Client Credentials only)

Integration Steps:
1. Register your application in the OAuth admin console
2. Configure redirect URIs
3. Implement PKCE challenge generation
4. Add token validation middleware to your service
5. Handle token refresh in your HTTP client

Security Considerations:
- Always use HTTPS
- Store tokens securely (HttpOnly cookies or secure storage)
- Validate token signature and claims on every request
- Implement token revocation for logout

title: Kubernetes Cluster Management Guide
author: Lisa Wang
content:
Kubernetes Cluster Management Guide

Cluster Overview:
- Provider: AWS EKS
- Version: 1.28
- Worker Nodes: 12 (m5.2xlarge)
- Namespaces: production, staging, monitoring, system

Node Management:
- Auto-scaling: Min 8, Max 16 nodes
- Node groups: general-purpose, memory-optimized
- Spot instances for non-critical workloads

Deployment Practices:
- All deployments via ArgoCD (GitOps)
- Helm charts for all applications
- Rolling updates with maxUnavailable: 1
- Readiness probes required for all pods

Monitoring Stack:
- Prometheus for metrics collection
- Grafana for dashboards
- Loki for log aggregation
- Jaeger for distributed tracing

Disaster Recovery:
- Daily etcd snapshots to S3
- Multi-AZ node distribution
- Automated failover for stateful services
- RTO: 30 minutes, RPO: 1 hour

title: Incident Response Procedure
author: Lisa Wang
content:
Incident Response Procedure

Severity Levels:
- P1 (Critical): Complete service outage, data loss risk
- P2 (High): Major feature unavailable, significant degradation
- P3 (Medium): Minor feature impact, workaround available
- P4 (Low): Cosmetic issues, non-urgent improvements

Response Times:
- P1: Acknowledge within 5 minutes, resolve within 1 hour
- P2: Acknowledge within 15 minutes, resolve within 4 hours
- P3: Acknowledge within 1 hour, resolve within 24 hours
- P4: Acknowledge within 4 hours, resolve within 1 week

Incident Workflow:
1. Detection (monitoring alert or user report)
2. Triage (determine severity and impact)
3. Communication (notify stakeholders via #incidents)
4. Investigation (identify root cause)
5. Resolution (apply fix or workaround)
6. Recovery (verify system stability)
7. Post-mortem (document lessons learned)

Communication Templates:
- Initial notification: '[P{level}] {title} - Impact: {description}'
- Update: '[UPDATE] {title} - Status: {investigating/identified/fixing}'
- Resolved: '[RESOLVED] {title} - Root cause: {cause}, Duration: {time}'
